issues.description: Spoken script says 'about five percent of a covalent bond' for H-bond strength without specifying energy vs length — a careful student may memorize this as a precise figure. Typical H-bond energy is ~5-10% of a covalent bond (roughly 5-30 kJ/mol vs ~350+ kJ/mol for C-C). Also the spoken version drops the 'NOT generic dipole-dipole' clarification that's in the outline content_hint — a top student watching only audio will not learn the donor/acceptor specificity is what distinguishes H-bonds from ordinary dipole-dipole.

Ionic bonds described as 'transfer electrons' is the classic intro-chem oversimplification. At the next level (gen chem, biochem), ionic bonding is better framed as strong electrostatic attraction between ions formed by large electronegativity differences — 'transfer' makes students think electrons are literally handed over as discrete events and creates problems when they encounter polar covalent on a continuum with ionic.

'Oxygen pulls electrons harder than hydrogen' is fine, but the lesson never explicitly defines or uses the word 'electronegativity' in the spoken script (it's only in the vocab list). A student who later sees 'electronegativity difference' on a FRQ may not connect it to this lesson.

The spoken script for functional groups DROPS the carbonyl group entirely and DROPS hydroxyl's role in sugars/serine. Outline lists 7 functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, amino, sulfhydryl, phosphate, methyl); spoken narration enumerates only 6 (skips carbonyl). Carbonyl (C=O) is on the AP CED and is essential for distinguishing aldoses vs ketoses and for understanding the peptide bond and aldehyde/ketone sugars. A student listening to the video and not reading slides will be blindsided by carbonyl on a test.

Isomers are mentioned but the three subtypes (structural, geometric/cis-trans, enantiomers) listed in the outline are NOT named in the spoken script. A student hears 'same formula, different structures' and will not be able to define enantiomers when asked — yet enantiomers (especially L-amino acids and D-sugars in biology) are testable.

Spoken script says carboxyl is 'negatively charged at physiological pH' and amino is 'positively charged' without explaining that these are the DEPROTONATED/PROTONATED forms (–COO⁻ and –NH₃⁺). A careful student looking at a textbook structure drawn as –COOH and –NH₂ will get confused about why the lesson said they're charged when the picture shows them neutral.

Lesson teaches dehydration synthesis as 'remove water to build' but never mentions that the water leaving group is specifically –OH from one monomer + –H from the other. Without this, a student cannot predict which atoms leave when shown two specific monomers (a common FRQ ask), and the asymmetric mechanism of glycosidic/peptide/phosphodiester bond formation feels arbitrary.

The spoken script collapses the polysaccharide treatment by dropping glycogen and chitin entirely (only starch and cellulose remain). Glycogen branching (α-1,6) is testable and is on the AP CED. Outline mentions them; narration does not. A student relying on audio misses glycogen as animal energy storage — they may default to thinking animals store glucose as starch.

Phospholipid description in spoken script says 'hydrophilic head plus two hydrophobic tails' but never uses the word 'amphipathic' aloud (it's in the outline). The amphipathic property is the exact vocabulary the AP exam uses; missing it from audio means students will not match the term when they see it.

Steroids are mentioned in the outline (cholesterol, testosterone, estrogen, 4 fused rings) but DROPPED from spoken script entirely. Steroids appear on AP Bio and the student listening will miss them as a lipid category.

Spoken script mentions tertiary is 'driven by R-group interactions' but does NOT enumerate the four specific interaction types (H-bonds, ionic, disulfide bridges, hydrophobic effect) that the outline lists. The hydrophobic-effect-is-not-a-bond nuance from the outline revision is completely lost in audio. A top student will not learn the exclusion-vs-bond distinction the revision was supposed to teach.

'Heat or pH can denature' — the outline also lists salt, and the spoken script omits it. More importantly, the spoken script does not explicitly state that denaturation causes LOSS OF FUNCTION — a critical AP exam point. It says 'shape unfolds and function is lost' is only in the outline narration_seed, not the final script.

Lesson never distinguishes the SUGAR difference (ribose has 2'-OH, deoxyribose has 2'-H) which is the molecular basis for DNA's stability vs RNA's reactivity. Also the spoken script does not state RNA bases (A, U, G, C) — only that 'RNA uses ribose, is usually single-stranded' is implied in outline but dropped in script. Students may not know U replaces T in RNA from the audio alone.

Phosphodiester bond described as 'between 3' hydroxyl of one sugar and 5' phosphate of the next' — correct, but a top student needs to know that DNA/RNA polymerases ADD nucleotides to the 3' end (synthesis direction 5'→3') because the incoming nucleotide's 5'-triphosphate attacks the existing 3'-OH. The 5'→3' directionality is stated but the WHY (and that it determines leading/lagging strand later) is not foreshadowed.

Recap claims 'water's polarity gives us six life-supporting properties' but only enumerates them in 06_water_properties — the recap does not re-list them, so a student reviewing only the recap cannot retrieve the six. Also recap drops the functional-group enumeration mentioned in the revision notes.

Script-vs-outline drift: the revision_notes describe many nuances added to the outline (donor/acceptor for H-bonds, hydrophobic-effect-as-exclusion, α-1,4 vs β-1,4, salt as denaturant, isomer subtypes, carbonyl, glycogen, steroids, amphipathic, RNA base set) — but a substantial fraction of these did NOT make it into the spoken script. A student watching the video without studying the slides will miss the very nuances the revision was designed to add.

summary: Lesson content is accurate and the outline handles common misconceptions well, but the spoken script drops several pieces of nuance the outline added (carbonyl group, isomer subtypes, glycogen/chitin/steroids, amphipathic, hydrophobic-effect-is-not-a-bond, RNA base set) — an audio-only learner will miss testable AP content, with the missing carbonyl group being the most exam-relevant gap.
